items.prompt_md: In the bottom-up `max_depth` recursion, what does a call on `root.left` **return** to its caller?
options: The depth of the left subtree, as an integer
A boolean saying whether the left subtree is balanced
Nothing — depth is tracked with a nonlocal counter instead
items.answer: The depth of the left subtree, as an integer
items.explanation_md: Each recursive call returns an integer — the height of the subtree rooted at that node — and the parent combines its two children's returned integers into its own: `1 + max(left, right)`.

items.prompt_md: You need a single aggregate number about a binary tree (a height, a longest-path length, a yes/no balance check), where the value at a node is a small combination of the same value at its children. Which approach fits?
options: Post-order recursion that returns a value per node, combining children's return values at the parent
A hash map keyed by node value to detect duplicates
A sliding window over a level-order flattening of the tree
Two pointers starting from the tree's minimum and maximum leaves
items.answer: Post-order recursion that returns a value per node, combining children's return values at the parent
items.explanation_md: Whenever the answer is defined recursively in terms of the same answer on the left and right subtrees, bottom-up (post-order) aggregation is the direct fit — compute the children's values first, then combine.

items.prompt_md: The one-pass diameter solution computes each node's depth and updates a running best diameter in the same post-order walk. What is its time complexity, versus calling a separate `max_depth()` at every node?
options: O(n) for the one-pass version, versus O(n^2) if depth is recomputed at every node
O(n log n) for both versions
O(n) for both versions — recomputing depth doesn't change the complexity
O(h) for the one-pass version, versus O(n) for the naive version
items.answer: O(n) for the one-pass version, versus O(n^2) if depth is recomputed at every node
items.explanation_md: Folding the depth computation and the diameter update into one post-order pass visits each node once — O(n). Calling a fresh depth() helper at every node during another walk redoes subtree work repeatedly, O(n) work at up to O(n) nodes — O(n^2).
